report:
NARRATIVE:
Exam: Chest 2 Views 4/5
 
Clinical History: 73 years Male with Rule out pneumonia
 
Comparison: 4/5/2010
 
IMPRESSION:
 
1.  PA AND LATERAL VIEWS OF THE CHEST DEMONSTRATE a nasogastric tube 
in place which can be followed in a subdiaphragmatic position.
 
2.  A left basilar opacity is seen involving the anterior/medial 
basal segments of the left lower lobe with a small left-sided pleural 
effusion.  Findings may represent infection versus aspiration.
 
3.  A trace right-sided pleural effusion is seen.  The right lung is 
otherwise grossly clear without focal air space opacity.
 
4.  Multilevel degenerative changes are seen throughout the thoracic 
spine with prominent osteophytosis.  There is mild anterior wedging 
of a lower thoracic vertebral body, favor chronic change, however 
recommend correlation for pain at this site. 
 
SUMMARY:4-POSSIBLY SIGNIFICANT FINDING, MAY NEED ACTION
I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed
with the report transcribed above.

consolidation.

report:
NARRATIVE:
338.480.262.3 TWO VIEW CHEST: 10-2-2006
0953331 SINGLE VIEW CHEST: 06-10-2
CLINICAL HISTORY: Follow up pneumothoraces. Status post biopsy of
cavitary lung nodules.
FINDINGS: Sequence of chest radiographs reveal stable placement of
a left PICC line in the superior vena cava. There is interval
removal of the left pigtail drain catheter, without development of
a left pneumothorax. There are multiple stable pulmonary nodules
and masses, most prominent in the right upper lobe and left lower
lobe, but others in the perihilar distribution as well. Fine
reticular opacities are also present bilaterally to a variable
extent, more prominent in the lower lobes on 10/2/2006 at 0900 and
on 10/2/2006 at 0900 hours. These could be some element of
evanescent pulmonary edema.
IMPRESSION:
1. OVERALL, RELATIVELY STABLE PULMONARY FINDINGS FOLLOWING REMOVAL
OF CHEST TUBE WITHOUT PNEUMOTHORAX.
2. MULTIPLE PULMONARY NODULES AND MASS, SOME CAVITARY, WITH
A WAXING AND WANING DIFFUSE RETICULAR PATTERN THAT MAY BE
UNDERLYING EDEMA.
END OF IMPRESSION:
SUMMARY:4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

report:
NARRATIVE:
CLINICAL HISTORY:   Kidney transplant candidate evaluation.
 FINDINGS:
 Two views of the chest 7/29/2007 are evaluated without prior
comparison study.  The heart appears moderately-to-markedly enlarged.
A left-sided central venous catheter with two lumens, likely a
hemodialysis catheter, is seen with distal tip at the level of the
right atrium.  There is perhaps minimal blunting of the costophrenic
sulci bilaterally.  There is no pneumothorax.  Pulmonary vasculature
appears slightly indistinct, particularly at the right mid to lower
lung zones.  There are no areas of dense parenchymal lobar
consolidation.  There is minimal fluid seen tracking in the right
major fissure and perhaps trace fluid at the right minor fissure.
 IMPRESSION:
 1.  CARDIOMEGALY.
 2.  TRACE PLEURAL EFFUSION AT THE RIGHT GREATER THAN LEFT LUNG BASE,
WITH SUGGESTION OF A FEW KERLEY B LINES AT THE RIGHT LUNG BASE WITH
SLIGHTLY INDISTINCT PULMONARY INTERSTITIAL MARKINGS.  THE
CONSTELLATION OF FINDINGS SUGGESTS SOME ELEMENT OF INTERSTITIAL
PULMONARY EDEMA, HOWEVER, AN ATYPICAL INFECTIOUS PROCESS OR OTHER
ETIOLOGY IS NOT EXCLUDED.  RECOMMEND CLINICAL CORRELATION AND
FOLLOW-UP IMAGING CHEST X-RAY IF CLINICALLY INDICATED.
 END OF IMPRESSION:
SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

perihilar region, suspicious for infection.

report:
NARRATIVE:
CHEST, TWO VIEWS:  4-26-2002 
 
COMPARISON:  4-26-2002. 
 
CLINICAL HISTORY:  Seventy-three-year-old man with fever, cough.  
Patient with also history of rheumatoid arthritis.  
 
FINDINGS:  Frontal and lateral projections of the chest taken on 
26/4 at 1706 hours demonstrate a normal size cardiac 
silhouette.  Aorta is mildly tortuous.  There is a stent likely in 
the proximal left subclavian artery, unchanged from prior study.  
Again noted is diffuse peripheral reticular opacity in both lungs, 
not significantly changed since one-year ago.  This finding suggests 
pulmonary fibrosis.  There are cystic lucencies in both lungs 
diffusely distributed which may represent emphysema.  On the frontal 
projection, there is no acute change the previous study to suggest a 
pneumonia.  On the lateral projection, there is an area of increased 
density projecting over the ascending aorta, anterior to a major 
fissure.  This is new from prior study.  But in the absence of 
corroboration on the frontal view, this finding may be artifactual.
 
IMPRESSION:
 
1.  EVIDENCE FOR CHRONIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS AS WELL AS EMPHYSEMA.  
THIS APPEARS TO STABLE BE SINCE 4-26-02. 
 
2.  NO DEFINITE EVIDENCE FOR ACUTE PNEUMONIA BY COMPARING FRONTAL 
VIEW.  A NEW AREA OF ANTERIOR PULMONARY OPACITY SEEN ONLY ON THE 
LATERAL VIEW MAY BE ARTIFACTUAL.  HOWEVER, IF PATIENT'S SYMPTOMS 
PERSIST, RECOMMEND REPEAT CHEST RADIOGRAPHS TO CONFIRM.
 
SUMMARY:4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

report:
NARRATIVE:
RADIOGRAPHIC EXAMINATION OF THE CHEST: 5-21-2010
 
CLINICAL HISTORY: 44 years of age, Female, Portable upright, sob, r/o 
pna, ptx.
 
COMPARISON: 5/21/10 and 21st May 10
PROCEDURE COMMENTS: Single view of the chest. 
 
FINDINGS:
 
There are low lung volumes. There is increased patchy opacity at the 
right lung base and in the upper perimediastinal regions bilaterally 
with prominent bronchopulmonary vascular markings. Findings could be 
suggestive of pneumonia or edema, especially with the findings of 
mild peribronchial thickening. Cardiac size is unchanged and slightly 
enlarged. No change in right pacemaker.
 
IMPRESSION:
 
1.  Low lung volumes and increased patchy opacities suggestive of 
pneumonia or edema, probably accentuated by the low lung volumes.
 
 
 
Physician to Radiologist Consult Line 931 603-1020

report:
NARRATIVE:
CHEST RADIOGRAPHS:  4-23-2004 
 
CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 69-year-old male with history of pancreatic 
mass.  
 
COMPARISON:  4/23/2004
 
FINDINGS:  Single frontal view of the chest obtained 04-23 at 
0036 hours demonstrates stable positioning of the support equipment.  
The left upper extremity PICC remains curled, likely within the 
azygos vein.  The side-port of the NG tube remains near the distal 
esophagus.  Cardiomediastinal silhouette is stable in configuration 
with mild cardiomegaly.  There has been interval increase in 
bilateral pleural effusions and bibasilar opacities.  Diffuse 
reticular and airspace opacities may reflect moderate edema.  
 
Subsequent single frontal view of the chest obtained 4/23/2004 at 
0534 hours demonstrates interval improved aeration of the lung bases.  
No other significant interval change.  Support equipment remains in 
unchanged position.  
 
IMPRESSION: 
 
1.  SERIES OF TWO CHEST RADIOGRAPHS DEMONSTRATE INTERVAL INCREASE IN 
BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS.  INCREASING BIBASILAR OPACITIES WITH 
DIFFUSE RETICULAR AND AIRSPACE OPACITIES MAY REFLECT MODERATE 
PULMONARY EDEMA.  STABLE CONFIRMATION OF THE CARDIOMEDIASTINAL 
SILHOUETTE WITH MILD CARDIOMEGALY.  
 
2.  SUPPORT EQUIPMENT REMAINS IN UNCHANGED POSITION, WITH THE LEFT 
UPPER EXTREMITY PICC LIKELY IN THE AZYGOS VEIN, AND THE NG SIDE-PORT 
NEAR THE DISTAL ESOPHAGUS, AS DESCRIBED ABOVE.  
 
SUMMARY:2-ABNORMAL, PREVIOUSLY REPORTED 
I have personally reviewed the images for this examination and agreed
with the report transcribed above.

pulmonary edema.  The heart is mildly enlarged.  There is a new, 
sharply-circumscribed density in the region of the right

pericardial cyst.  Calcified mediastinal lymph nodes are noted.

WHICH MAY REPRESENT A FAT PAD OR POSSIBLY AN EPICARDIAL-PERICARDIAL 
CYST.

report:
NARRATIVE:
CHEST X-RAY:  1/5/2015
COMPARISON:  1/5/2015.
CLINICAL INFORMATION:  Acute on chronic renal failure.  Short of
breath with blood transfusion.
IMPRESSION:
1.  AP SEMIERECT CHEST RADIOGRAPH DEMONSTRATES A LEFT-SIDED AICD
PACEMAKER, WITH THE PACING DEVICE NOT VISUALIZED ON THE FILM.
2.  STERNOTOMY WIRES ARE PRESENT, WITH ASSOCIATED MEDIASTINAL CLIPS.
THE HEART SIZE IS WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS.  STABLE CALCIFICATION IS SEEN
WITHIN THE AORTIC KNOB.
3.   MILD CEPHALIZATION IS PRESENT, WITH HAZY OPACIFICATION IN THE
MID AND LOWER ZONES BILATERALLY, CONCERNING FOR EARLY INTERSTITIAL
PULMONARY EDEMA.  THERE IS MORE FOCAL OPACIFICATION AT THE LEFT BASE,
WITH LIKELY SMALL BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS.
4.  STABLE ELEVATION OF THE LEFT HEMIDIAPHRAGM, WITH PROMINENT GAS
FILLED LOOPS OF COLON IN THE REGION OF THE SPLENIC FLEXURE.
5.  THERE IS ABNORMAL BONE DENSITY WITHIN THE LEFT HUMERAL HEAD, WITH
PROMINENT TRABECULATION AND MILD CORTICAL THICKENING.  THE
APPEARANCES ARE SUGGESTIVE OF UNDERLYING PAGET'S DISEASE.
SUMMARY:4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION
